In Maori belief, this is Te Rerenga Wairua — the leaping-off place of spirits. When a person dies, their spirit travels the length of New Zealand to this point, descends the roots of an ancient pohutukawa tree clinging to the headland, and departs for the ancestral homeland of Hawaiki. Below you, the Tasman Sea and Pacific Ocean collide in a dramatic churning of currents visible from the clifftop. The lighthouse, built in 1941, stands on a narrow promontory 165 metres above the water. This is a sacred place — please treat it with respect and stay on the path. The sense of standing at the very edge of a land is powerful and unforgettable.

reviews.summary: The Cape Reinga Lighthouse Walk route delivers a memorable journey through exceptional New Zealand scenery. Walk to New Zealand's northernmost lighthouse where two oceans meet and spirits depart. Visitors recommend allowing extra time to stop at viewpoints — reviewers consistently say the journey itself is as rewarding as the destination.

pois.name: Puketi Forest Nature Trail
pois.short: Walk through an ancient kauri and podocarp forest that doubles as a nocturnal kiwi sanctuary.
pois.commentary: Puketi Forest is a 21,000-hectare tract of native bush between the Bay of Islands and Hokianga. It contains significant stands of mature kauri and is one of the best places in Northland to encounter wild kiwi at night. The Manginangina Kauri Walk is a boardwalk loop through a cathedral-like stand of kauri.
pois.audio_story: You are entering Puketi Forest, 21,000 hectares of ancient bush that sits quietly between the tourist hubs of the Bay of Islands and the Hokianga. The Manginangina Kauri Walk takes you on a boardwalk through a stand of kauri trees so tall they create a natural cathedral. Sunlight filters through the canopy in shafts, and the air smells of damp earth and tree resin. If you return at dusk, this is one of the best places in Northland to hear — and possibly see — wild North Island brown kiwi foraging in the undergrowth. Guided night walks operate here, and the experience of hearing that distinctive high-pitched call in the darkness is genuinely thrilling. Remember to clean your footwear at the hygiene stations to help protect the kauri from dieback disease.

reviews.summary: The Puketi Forest Nature Trail route delivers a memorable journey through exceptional New Zealand scenery. Walk through an ancient kauri and podocarp forest that doubles as a nocturnal kiwi sanctuary. Visitors recommend allowing extra time to stop at viewpoints — reviewers consistently say the journey itself is as rewarding as the destination.

pois.name: Whangarei Falls and Hatea River Walk
pois.short: Walk to Whangarei's iconic waterfall and along the Hatea River through native bush and sculpture gardens.
pois.commentary: Whangarei Falls drops 26 metres in a single plunge into a bush-fringed pool. The Hatea River walk extends from the falls through A.H. Reed Memorial Park and along the river to the Town Basin marina. The walk passes kauri trees, a canopy walkway, and the Quarry Arts Centre.
pois.audio_story: You are approaching Whangarei Falls through native bush, and the sound of falling water builds with every step. The falls drop 26 metres in a single photogenic plunge into a deep pool surrounded by ferns and overhanging trees. This is one of the most visited waterfalls in Northland, and the viewpoints from both the top and bottom of the falls are excellent. From here, the Hatea River walk extends downstream through A.H. Reed Memorial Park, named after the Northland publisher and conservationist. The park features a canopy walkway through native bush and some fine kauri trees. Continue along the river through sculpture gardens and past the Quarry Arts Centre to the Town Basin marina, where cafes and galleries line the waterfront. The entire walk is about 5 kilometres and follows well-maintained paths. It is a surprisingly rewarding urban walk that links Whangarei's best natural and cultural features in a single route.

reviews.summary: The Whangarei Falls and Hatea River Walk route delivers a memorable journey through exceptional New Zealand scenery. Walk to Whangarei's iconic waterfall and along the Hatea River through native bush and sculpture gardens. Visitors recommend allowing extra time to stop at viewpoints — reviewers consistently say the journey itself is as rewarding as the destination.

pois.name: Bay of Islands Parasailing
pois.short: Parasail 400 feet above the 144 islands of the Bay of Islands.
pois.commentary: Parasailing from Paihia lifts riders up to 400 feet above the Bay of Islands, revealing the full panorama of 144 subtropical islands. The calm, sheltered waters of the bay provide reliable conditions. Dolphin sightings from the air are common.
pois.audio_story: You have arrived at the Paihia wharf, where a parasailing boat is ready to take you to a vantage point few people ever experience. As the boat accelerates across the Bay of Islands and the canopy fills with air, you will rise smoothly to 400 feet above the water. From this height, the full majesty of the Bay of Islands reveals itself, all 144 islands scattered across turquoise water like green jewels on blue silk. You might spot dolphins playing in the wake of passing boats, or see stingrays cruising the sandy shallows near the shore. The bay is one of the most historically significant places in New Zealand, it was here at Waitangi, visible to your right, that the Treaty was signed in 1840. The flight is surprisingly peaceful at altitude, just you and one of the most beautiful coastal landscapes on Earth.

reviews.summary: The Bay of Islands Parasailing adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Parasail 400 feet above the 144 islands of the Bay of Islands. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

pois.name: Poor Knights Islands Diving
pois.short: Dive one of the world's top 10 dive sites at the Poor Knights Islands marine reserve.
pois.commentary: The Poor Knights Islands, rated by Jacques Cousteau as one of the world's top 10 dive sites, sit 23 km off the Northland coast. The marine reserve features sea caves, arches, and the world's largest sea cave, Rikoriko. Subtropical currents bring exceptional marine diversity and visibility up to 30 metres.
pois.audio_story: You are heading out to the Poor Knights Islands, a place that Jacques Cousteau himself ranked among the top 10 dive sites in the world. These ancient volcanic islands sit 23 kilometres off the coast near Tutukaka, and their underwater landscape is nothing short of extraordinary. The islands drop away into vertical walls that plummet into the depths, covered in sponge gardens, colourful anemones, and forests of black coral. Subtropical currents from the Coral Sea bring warm water and an astonishing diversity of marine life, from nudibranchs smaller than your fingernail to bronze whaler sharks and manta rays. You will dive through arches, swim-throughs, and Rikoriko Cave, the world's largest sea cave, which is so vast that a passenger ship once motored inside. Visibility regularly exceeds 30 metres, making every dive feel like floating in liquid air.

reviews.summary: The Poor Knights Islands Diving adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Dive one of the world's top 10 dive sites at the Poor Knights Islands marine reserve. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

pois.name: Te Paki Sand Dunes Sandboarding
pois.short: Sandboard down massive dunes at the tip of New Zealand near Cape Reinga.
pois.commentary: The Te Paki sand dunes near Cape Reinga are some of the largest in the Southern Hemisphere, rising up to 150 metres. Sandboarding or body-boarding down the steep faces is a classic Northland adventure. Boards can be hired from operators at the stream crossing or brought from Kaitaia.
pois.audio_story: You have arrived at the Te Paki stream, the gateway to some of the most spectacular sand dunes in the Southern Hemisphere. These massive dunes rise up to 150 metres and stretch for several kilometres near the very tip of New Zealand. Wade across the shallow stream and climb the dunes, and the scale of them becomes almost surreal, pure golden sand sculpted into vast, wind-shaped ridges with barely a footprint in sight. Sandboarding down the steep faces is an absolute rush, you can reach surprising speeds, and the landing is always soft. You do not need any experience, just wax the bottom of your board for speed, point it downhill, and let gravity do the rest. The walk back up is the hard part, but the ride down makes every step worthwhile. These dunes have been here for thousands of years, constantly shifting and reshaping with the wind.

reviews.summary: The Te Paki Sand Dunes Sandboarding adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Sandboard down massive dunes at the tip of New Zealand near Cape Reinga. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

pois.name: Waitangi Mountain Bike Park
pois.short: Ride flowy singletrack through native bush in the Waitangi Mountain Bike Park.
pois.commentary: The Waitangi Mountain Bike Park offers over 30 kilometres of trails through native bush and pine forest behind the Treaty Grounds. Trails range from beginner flow to expert-grade downhill, with features including berms, jumps, and technical root sections. Free to ride.
pois.audio_story: You have arrived at the Waitangi Mountain Bike Park, a trail network that has transformed the hills behind one of New Zealand's most historically significant sites into a riding destination. Over 30 kilometres of trails wind through a mix of native bush and pine forest, with options for every ability. Beginners will love the smooth flow trails with gentle berms and rollers, while advanced riders can tackle steep, rooty descents and technical rock gardens. The park has been built and maintained by a dedicated local club, and the quality of the trail building is superb, with well-shaped berms, tabletop jumps, and creative features throughout. Best of all, it is completely free to ride. The Bay of Islands is visible from several high points on the trail network. After your ride, the Treaty Grounds are just down the hill if you want to combine adventure with a dose of New Zealand history.

reviews.summary: The Waitangi Mountain Bike Park adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Ride flowy singletrack through native bush in the Waitangi Mountain Bike Park. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

pois.name: Bay of Islands Sea Kayaking
pois.short: Kayak between subtropical islands and encounter dolphins in the Bay of Islands.
pois.commentary: Sea kayaking in the Bay of Islands offers island hopping across sheltered subtropical waters. Multi-island tours visit Roberton Island (Motuarohia), Waewaetorea, and secluded beaches only accessible from the water. Dolphin encounters are common, with a resident pod of bottlenose dolphins.
pois.audio_story: You have arrived at the launching point for a sea kayaking adventure through one of the most beautiful coastal environments in New Zealand. The Bay of Islands contains 144 islands spread across a vast harbour system, and a kayak is the perfect way to explore them at your own pace. Your route will take you across sheltered channels to islands with deserted white sand beaches, through narrow passages between rocky outcrops, and into secluded coves that no tour boat can reach. Keep your eyes on the water, this bay is home to a resident pod of bottlenose dolphins, and they are famously curious about kayakers. You might also spot penguins, stingrays, and eagle rays in the clear shallows. Roberton Island, with its twin lagoons visible from the hilltop, is a highlight that Captain Cook himself admired when he first visited in 1769.

reviews.summary: The Bay of Islands Sea Kayaking adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Kayak between subtropical islands and encounter dolphins in the Bay of Islands. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

pois.name: Tane Mahuta Giant Kauri Walk
pois.short: Stand before Tane Mahuta, the largest living kauri tree and lord of the forest.
pois.commentary: Tane Mahuta in the Waipoua Forest is the largest living kauri tree, standing 51.2 metres tall with a trunk girth of 13.77 metres. The short boardwalk from the road takes only 5 minutes. The tree is estimated to be between 1,250 and 2,500 years old. Shoe-cleaning stations help prevent kauri dieback disease.
pois.audio_story: You have arrived at the boardwalk entrance to Tane Mahuta, the Lord of the Forest. In just five minutes of gentle walking, you will stand before the largest living kauri tree on Earth, and nothing can prepare you for the scale of it. Tane Mahuta rises 51.2 metres into the canopy, its trunk measuring 13.77 metres in girth, a column of living wood that began growing somewhere between 1,250 and 2,500 years ago. This tree was already ancient when the first Polynesian voyagers arrived in New Zealand. In Maori mythology, Tane Mahuta is the god of the forest who separated his parents, Ranginui the sky father and Papatuanuku the earth mother, to bring light into the world. Before you step onto the boardwalk, please use the shoe-cleaning station to help prevent kauri dieback disease, a pathogen that threatens these magnificent trees. Treat this visit with the reverence it deserves.

reviews.summary: The Tane Mahuta Giant Kauri Walk adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Stand before Tane Mahuta, the largest living kauri tree and lord of the forest. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

pois.name: Ahipara Quad Bike / ATV Tours
pois.short: Blast along Ninety Mile Beach and through the dunes on a powerful ATV.
pois.commentary: ATV tours from Ahipara take riders along the southern end of Ninety Mile Beach and into the spectacular Tauroa Point dune system. Tours combine beach riding with dune exploration, offering a high-energy way to experience this remote coastline. No experience necessary.
pois.audio_story: You have arrived in Ahipara, the southern gateway to Ninety Mile Beach, and an ATV is about to take you on an unforgettable ride along one of the longest beaches in New Zealand. Despite its name, the beach is actually 88 kilometres long, but that is still an extraordinary stretch of uninterrupted sand. Your quad bike tour begins on the hard-packed sand near the waterline, where you can open the throttle and feel the salt spray on your face. Then you will head into the Tauroa Point dune system, a landscape of towering sand hills, hidden valleys, and views that stretch to the Tasman Sea. The dunes are constantly changing shape, carved by the prevailing westerly winds. No experience is needed, the guides will show you everything, and the bikes are powerful but manageable. Just be prepared for sand in places you did not know sand could reach.

reviews.summary: The Ahipara Quad Bike / ATV Tours adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Blast along Ninety Mile Beach and through the dunes on a powerful ATV. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

pois.name: Whangarei Heads Rock Climbing
pois.short: Climb volcanic rock with ocean views at the crags near Whangarei Heads.
pois.commentary: The Whangarei Heads area offers sport climbing on volcanic rock with stunning coastal views. Routes range from beginner to advanced across several crags. The Parahaki and Whangarei Falls areas also have climbing options. The region's climbing is documented by the NZAC.
pois.audio_story: You have arrived at the Whangarei Heads climbing area, where volcanic rock faces meet the Pacific Ocean in a setting that makes every route feel like an expedition. The crags here offer sport climbing on solid basalt and andesite, with routes ranging from gentle slabs for beginners to overhanging test-pieces for seasoned climbers. What sets this climbing apart is the view, from the higher routes you can see across Whangarei Harbour, out to the Poor Knights Islands, and along a coastline of volcanic headlands and sandy bays. The rock is generally well-featured with positive holds, though some routes require careful footwork on small edges. The climbing community in Northland is small but passionate, and the local NZAC section has documented the routes thoroughly. Bring your own gear, there are no guide services here, but the access is straightforward and the rock speaks for itself. The afternoon sea breeze keeps the temperature comfortable even in summer.

reviews.summary: The Whangarei Heads Rock Climbing adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Climb volcanic rock with ocean views at the crags near Whangarei Heads. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

pois.name: Mangawhai Cliff Walk
pois.short: Walk spectacular coastal cliffs with views to the Hen and Chicken Islands.
pois.commentary: The Mangawhai Cliff Walk is a 5 km loop track along dramatic coastal cliffs north of Auckland. The track passes through pohutukawa forest and scrubland with views to the Hen and Chicken Islands. A circuit includes the beach return at low tide.
pois.audio_story: You have arrived at the start of the Mangawhai Cliff Walk, one of the finest coastal walks in Northland and a trail that delivers dramatic scenery for relatively little effort. The 5-kilometre track follows the cliff edge above a wild, wind-sculpted coastline, with views stretching out to the Hen and Chicken Islands on the horizon. The path winds through groves of pohutukawa trees, New Zealand's iconic coastal native, which explode into crimson bloom every December. Along the way, you will pass above rocky coves where the ocean surges into sea caves, and across grassy headlands where the wind is fresh and the views are long. At the far end, the track descends to the beach, and at low tide you can walk back along the sand to complete a satisfying loop. Keep an eye on the ocean, this coast catches big swells from the northeast, and the waves breaking against the cliffs are mesmerising. The walk is free and well maintained, suitable for anyone with reasonable fitness.

reviews.summary: The Mangawhai Cliff Walk adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Walk spectacular coastal cliffs with views to the Hen and Chicken Islands. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

pois.name: MĀHA Restaurant
pois.short: Kerikeri's acclaimed Asian-influenced restaurant — subtropical gardens, local produce and creative cuisine.
pois.commentary: Set in the lush subtropical gardens of Wharepuke, MĀHA delivers one of Northland's most refined dining experiences. The menu draws on Asian flavours and techniques, using locally sourced ingredients — think Kerikeri citrus, locally caught fish and subtropical produce. The garden setting is beautiful, and the BYO wine policy makes it great value for the quality. Booking is essential, especially in summer.

reviews.summary: Foodies call this Northland's best restaurant. The Asian-influenced dishes are creative and well-executed. The garden setting enhances the experience. BYO wine is a bonus.

pois.name: Old Packhouse Market
pois.short: Weekend markets in Kerikeri's historic packhouse — Saturday produce, Sunday arts and crafts, 60-100 vendors.
pois.commentary: The Old Packhouse in Kerikeri hosts one of Northland's best markets. Saturdays focus on food — subtropical fruits, organic vegetables, artisan cheeses, fresh bread and local honey from 60-100 vendors. Sundays shift to arts and crafts with a more eclectic mix. The old citrus packhouse setting has real character, and the coffee and street-food stalls make it a leisurely morning out. Peak summer is the best time.

reviews.summary: Great variety of local produce, especially subtropical fruits. The Saturday market is better for food lovers. Can get crowded in peak summer.

pois.name: Fat Camel Cafe
pois.short: Whangarei waterfront café — authentic Israeli food, great coffee and a harbour setting.
pois.commentary: Fat Camel brings Middle Eastern flavours to Whangarei's Town Basin waterfront. The pita pockets stuffed with shawarma meat, falafel and fresh salads are the stars, alongside authentic Israeli dishes and excellent coffee. The waterfront setting overlooking the marina is pleasant, and it's a great lunch stop between Auckland and the Bay of Islands. Family-friendly with generous portions.

reviews.summary: The Middle Eastern food is authentic and well-priced. The waterfront location adds to the experience. Some find parking at the Town Basin tricky on busy days.

pois.name: Karikari Estate
pois.short: New Zealand's northernmost winery — estate wines, vineyard restaurant and Karikari Peninsula views.
pois.commentary: Karikari Estate is one of New Zealand's most remote wineries, perched on the Karikari Peninsula in the Far North. The warm subtropical climate produces distinctive wines — the Pinotage (a rarity in NZ) and Syrah are standouts. The vineyard restaurant serves fresh local produce with panoramic views. It's the kind of place you discover on a road trip and never forget. Call ahead to confirm opening hours.

reviews.summary: The remote location and unusual grape varieties create a memorable experience. The restaurant is good. Opening hours can be limited outside summer — check ahead.

pois.name: Marsden Estate Winery
pois.short: Bay of Islands winery with a cellar door restaurant — Chardonnay and Pinot Gris in subtropical Northland.
pois.commentary: Marsden Estate is the Bay of Islands' main winery, producing aromatic whites and Bordeaux-style reds in Northland's subtropical climate. The cellar door restaurant serves lunch among the vines with a menu built around local produce — Kerikeri avocados, fresh seafood, subtropical salads. The Black Rocks Chardonnay and Pinot Gris are the picks. A pleasant stop on the way between Kerikeri and Paihia.

reviews.summary: A pleasant surprise for visitors who don't expect wine in the Far North. The restaurant and setting are nice. Some note the wines don't match southern regions for complexity, but they're enjoyable.

pois.name: Kiwi Kai
pois.short: Traditional Māori kai in Whangarei — hāngī, kina, fried bread, fish and chips at the Cameron Street shop.
pois.commentary: Kiwi Kai on Cameron Street is a rare chance to try authentic Māori cuisine without booking a cultural experience. The hāngī (earth-oven cooked meat and vegetables), rewena bread (Māori sourdough), kina (sea urchin) and boil-up are all on the menu alongside excellent fish and chips. It's unpretentious — more takeaway shop than restaurant — but the food is the real deal and the prices are very reasonable.

reviews.summary: A unique opportunity to try authentic Māori kai. The hāngī and fried bread are highlights. Basic setting — this is about the food, not the ambiance.

pois.name: The Quay Kitchen
pois.short: Whangarei Town Basin waterfront dining — creative brunch and dinner menus with harbour views.
pois.commentary: The Quay Kitchen sits on the Whangarei Town Basin waterfront with a menu that runs from creative brunches through to dinner. The kitchen focuses on local produce with options for vegans and gluten-free diners. The harbour-side terrace is the place to be on a warm evening, watching boats come and go. Good coffee and a well-chosen NZ wine list complete the package.

reviews.summary: The waterfront setting is the main draw. The food is creative and accommodating of dietary needs. Some find dinner service inconsistent on busy nights.

pois.name: Charlotte's Kitchen
pois.short: Bay of Islands waterfront dining — fresh local seafood and NZ wine overlooking Paihia wharf.
pois.commentary: Charlotte's Kitchen occupies a prime waterfront spot in Paihia with views across the Bay of Islands. The menu majors on fresh local seafood — snapper, blue cod, green-lipped mussels and whatever's just off the boats — alongside NZ wines and craft beers. The sunset dinner on the terrace is a Bay of Islands highlight. Also does an excellent brunch. Book ahead in summer.

reviews.summary: The fresh seafood and views earn praise. A go-to for Bay of Islands dining. Can feel overrun with tourists in peak summer, but the food quality holds.

pois.name: The Duke of Marlborough
pois.short: NZ's oldest licensed hotel (est. 1827) — waterfront dining in historic Russell on the Bay of Islands.
pois.commentary: The Duke of Marlborough claims to be New Zealand's oldest pub, with its first licence granted in 1840. The waterfront restaurant in Russell serves excellent seafood and NZ cuisine with views across the Bay of Islands. The heritage is palpable — Kororāreka (Russell's original name) was once the 'hell-hole of the Pacific.' Today it's considerably more refined, but the Duke retains its character. Arrive by ferry from Paihia for the full experience.

reviews.summary: The history and waterfront setting are special. The food quality has improved in recent years. Some find the historic-pub prices steep.

pois.name: Sage Restaurant
pois.short: Kerikeri fine dining — local and foraged ingredients in a smart, contemporary setting.
pois.commentary: Sage has elevated Kerikeri's dining scene with a focus on local and foraged ingredients. The menu reflects what's available — expect subtropical fruits, wild herbs, fresh seafood and locally raised meats prepared with refined technique. The wine list features NZ producers, and the contemporary dining room is a pleasant surprise in this small Far North town. Booking recommended, especially in summer.

reviews.summary: Kerikeri's best fine-dining experience. The local focus is genuine and interesting. Limited seating means booking ahead is wise.

pois.name: Ake Ake Vineyard
pois.short: Kerikeri boutique vineyard — cellar door and restaurant set among subtropical gardens and vines.
pois.commentary: Ake Ake (named after the native timber) is a charming Kerikeri vineyard producing wines from tropical-climate grapes. The cellar door and restaurant sit among lush subtropical gardens, and the menu uses local Northland produce — avocados, citrus, fresh seafood. The Syrah and Pinotage show what's possible in Northland's warm climate. A peaceful stop on a Bay of Islands food trail.

reviews.summary: The subtropical garden setting is beautiful. The wines are interesting for their warm-climate character. Some find the restaurant hours limited.

pois.name: Poor Knights Islands Marine Reserve
pois.short: World top-10 dive site — subtropical fish, giant kelp forests, and massive cave systems.
pois.commentary: The Poor Knights Islands Marine Reserve is consistently rated among the world's top ten dive sites. Subtropical currents bring warm water and exotic species rarely seen elsewhere in NZ. Massive archways, cave systems, and kelp forests support incredible biodiversity. No landing permitted — diving and snorkelling only.

reviews.summary: Bucket-list diving. Even snorkellers are blown away by the fish life. Several operators from Tutukaka.

pois.name: Tiritiri Matangi Island — Open Sanctuary
pois.short: NZ's most successful open sanctuary — tākahē, kōkako, hihi, and little spotted kiwi on a ferry-accessible island.
pois.commentary: Tiritiri Matangi is the gold standard of NZ island restoration. Community volunteers planted 300,000 trees, and now endangered species including tākahē, kōkako, hihi (stitchbird), and little spotted kiwi thrive. A ferry from Auckland's Gulf Harbour makes it easily accessible for day trips.

reviews.summary: Best wildlife day trip from Auckland. The birds are incredibly tame. Guided walks highly recommended. Ferry books out.

pois.name: Waipoua Forest — Kiwi Night Walk
pois.short: Night walks among the ancient kauri to see North Island brown kiwi — guided by local Māori.
pois.commentary: Footprints Waipoua runs evening guided walks through Waipoua Forest, home of Tāne Mahuta, to see North Island brown kiwi in the wild. The Māori guides share cultural stories of the kauri and the forest. Kiwi sighting rates are very high due to the healthy population here.

reviews.summary: Unforgettable experience. The Māori-guided cultural connection makes it special. Kiwi sightings near-guaranteed.

pois.name: Bay of Islands — Dolphin Swimming
pois.short: Swim with bottlenose dolphins in the warm waters of the Bay of Islands — NZ's most popular dolphin encounter.
pois.commentary: The Bay of Islands is home to a resident pod of bottlenose dolphins. Several operators run swim-with-dolphin trips from Paihia and Russell, with high sighting rates year-round. The warm water and beautiful island scenery add to the experience.

reviews.summary: Popular for good reason. Dolphin encounters are magical. Operators are professional and conservation-minded.

pois.name: Marsden Cross — Little Blue Penguin Colony
pois.short: Little blue penguins nest among the rocks at Rangihoua Bay near the historic Marsden Cross.
pois.commentary: The rocky coastline at Rangihoua Bay near the historic Marsden Cross site hosts a small colony of little blue penguins. The combination of historical significance and wildlife makes this a unique stop. Dusk visits during breeding season offer the best viewing.

reviews.summary: Combines history and wildlife. Penguin viewing requires patience and good timing. Beautiful bay.

pois.name: Bream Head Scenic Reserve — Seabird Colony
pois.short: Headland reserve with grey-faced petrel colonies and spectacular Hen and Chickens Islands views.
pois.commentary: Bream Head is a rugged headland east of Whangarei with significant grey-faced petrel breeding colonies. The track to the summit is steep but rewarding, with panoramic views over the coast and to the Hen and Chickens Islands. Petrel activity is best at dusk during breeding season.

reviews.summary: Challenging but rewarding walk. Great seabird viewing. The views from the top are outstanding.

pois.name: Urupukapuka Island — Pest-Free Haven
pois.short: Predator-free island in the Bay of Islands — walking tracks, beaches, and native birds.
pois.commentary: Urupukapuka Island, the largest island in the Bay of Islands, has been cleared of predators. Walking tracks cross the island through regenerating bush where North Island robin, bellbird, and dotterel thrive. Sandy beaches and clear snorkelling waters add to the appeal.

reviews.summary: Great day trip from Paihia. Easy walking with good bird variety. The beaches are beautiful.

pois.name: Trounson Kauri Park — Giant Snail & Kiwi
pois.short: Night walks reveal kiwi and Kauri giant snail — Northland's nocturnal treasures in ancient forest.
pois.commentary: Trounson Kauri Park is a mainland island where intensive pest control has boosted kiwi numbers. DOC-guided night walks reveal North Island brown kiwi and the endemic Kauri snail (Paryphanta busbyi), one of the world's largest land snails. The ancient kauri trees are spectacular.

reviews.summary: Excellent night walk. Kiwi sightings are likely. The kauri snails are fascinatingly large. Book ahead.

pois.name: Mangawhai Heads — NZ Fairy Tern Colony
pois.short: One of the last breeding sites of the critically endangered NZ fairy tern — fewer than 40 remain.
pois.commentary: Mangawhai Heads is one of only four breeding sites for the NZ fairy tern, the country's rarest bird with fewer than 40 individuals. DOC wardens protect nests during the breeding season (Oct-Feb). NZ dotterel also breed here. Observe from marked viewing areas only.

reviews.summary: Privilege to see NZ's rarest bird. Follow all DOC signage. The beach and sandspit are beautiful.

pois.name: Karikari Peninsula — Dotterel & Shore Plover
pois.short: Remote Northland peninsula with significant NZ dotterel nesting beaches and rare shore plover sightings.
pois.commentary: The Karikari Peninsula's remote sandy beaches provide important nesting habitat for NZ dotterel. The dune-backed beaches are also occasional habitat for the rare shore plover. Community volunteer groups monitor and protect nests during the breeding season.

reviews.summary: Remote and uncrowded. Great for dotterel observation. The beaches are stunning. Keep dogs on leads.

pois.name: Whangarei Heads — Backyard Kiwi
pois.short: Community conservation success — kiwi now call in suburban backyards at Whangarei Heads.
pois.commentary: The Backyard Kiwi project at Whangarei Heads is one of NZ's great community conservation success stories. Intensive trapping by local volunteers has boosted kiwi numbers so dramatically that residents regularly hear them calling at night from their homes.

reviews.summary: Inspiring community conservation. Hearing kiwi from a suburban garden is surreal. Best at night in winter.

pois.name: Russell Forest — Bush Bird Haven
pois.short: Native forest walks behind Russell with kaka, tui, kereru, and North Island robin.
pois.commentary: The Russell Forest behind the historic Bay of Islands township offers excellent bush birding on well-maintained tracks. Intensive pest control has brought back kaka, tui in large numbers, kereru, and North Island robin. The Cape Brett track starts from this forest.

reviews.summary: Surprisingly good birding. The forest is lush and atmospheric. Multiple track options for different fitness levels.

pois.name: Matakohe Limestone Island — Wildlife Refuge
pois.short: Small predator-free island in Whangarei Harbour — banded rail, NZ robin, and red-crowned kakariki.
pois.commentary: Matakohe Limestone Island in Whangarei Harbour has been predator-free since 2002. Banded rail, North Island robin, red-crowned kakariki, and pateke (brown teal) have been released. The community-run Friends of Matakohe-Limestone Island run regular guided visits by kayak or boat.

reviews.summary: Great community conservation. The guided visits are well run. Kayak access adds to the adventure.

pois.name: Spirits Bay / Piwhane — Coastal Wildlife
pois.short: Remote beach at the tip of NZ — NZ dotterel, fur seals, and dolphins in pristine waters.
pois.commentary: Spirits Bay near Cape Reinga is one of NZ's most remote and pristine beaches. NZ dotterel nest on the dunes, NZ fur seals haul out on the headlands, and bottlenose dolphins frequent the bay. The remoteness and cultural significance (it's near the spirits' leaping place) make it special.

reviews.summary: Truly remote and beautiful. Self-contained camping available. The wildlife encounters feel authentic.

pois.name: Maitai Bay — Reef Snorkelling
pois.short: Warm, clear water snorkelling over reef systems — subtropical fish species at their southern limit.
pois.commentary: Maitai Bay in the Karikari Peninsula area offers some of Northland's best snorkelling. The rocky reef shelters subtropical species rarely seen further south, including Lord Howe coral fish and mosaic leatherjacket. The water is among NZ's warmest.

reviews.summary: Excellent snorkelling in warm water. The fish variety surprises many visitors. Best in summer.

pois.name: Tutukaka Coast Marine Reserve
pois.short: Gateway to the Poor Knights Islands — mainland reefs with diverse marine life and dive operators.
pois.commentary: The Tutukaka Coast serves as the gateway to the Poor Knights Islands Marine Reserve, with several dive operators based here. The mainland coast itself offers excellent shore diving and snorkelling at spots like Matapōuri and Whale Bay. Marine life is abundant.

reviews.summary: Great base for Poor Knights diving. Mainland snorkelling is also excellent. Multiple operators to choose from.

pois.name: Aroha Island Ecological Centre
pois.short: Kiwi listening and night walks on a predator-controlled peninsula in Kerikeri Inlet.
pois.commentary: Aroha Island near Kerikeri is a community-run ecological centre on a predator-controlled peninsula. Night walks offer excellent chances of hearing — and sometimes seeing — North Island brown kiwi. The centre also has a small wetland with fernbird and banded rail.

reviews.summary: Reliable kiwi encounters. The guides are knowledgeable and passionate. Camping and cabins available.

pois.name: Hundertwasser Art Centre Whangārei
pois.short: Stunning gallery housing Friedensreich Hundertwasser artworks in NZ's only building designed by the visionary artist.
pois.commentary: The Hundertwasser Art Centre in Whangārei's Town Basin is the last building designed by the legendary Austrian-New Zealand artist and architect Friedensreich Hundertwasser before his death in 2000. Opened in 2022 after decades of community campaigning and fundraising, the building transforms a former 1930s commercial building into a characteristically Hundertwasser creation — irregular forms, tree tenants growing from the structure, golden onion domes, colourful ceramic columns, and an undulating grass rooftop. Inside, the gallery houses the largest permanent collection of Hundertwasser's original works in the southern hemisphere, alongside temporary exhibitions. Hundertwasser lived in the Bay of Islands from 1973 until his death, and the centre celebrates his deep connection to New Zealand. The building is Australasia's most significant example of Hundertwasser architecture. Located in the attractive Town Basin waterfront precinct with cafés and other galleries. Allow two hours for the full experience.

reviews.summary: An extraordinary building and gallery — worth travelling to Whangārei just for this. The architecture is joyful and the artworks are fascinating. A world-class attraction for Northland.

pois.name: Kauri Museum Matakohe
pois.short: Outstanding museum dedicated to the mighty kauri tree and the pioneering logging and gum-digging industries.
pois.commentary: The Kauri Museum in Matakohe is one of New Zealand's finest regional museums, dedicated to telling the complete story of the kauri tree — from ancient forest ecology to the logging and gum-digging industries that shaped Northland's history. The museum houses an enormous collection including massive cross-sections of ancient kauri trees, a full-sized replica of a pioneer boarding house, a working steam sawmill model, and the world's largest collection of kauri gum in every conceivable form and colour. The displays bring to life the extraordinary world of the Northland bush camps, gum fields, and timber mills with remarkable detail and authenticity. Interactive exhibits and personal stories make the history accessible and engaging. The museum is set in the small rural town of Matakohe on State Highway 12, roughly midway between Auckland and the Bay of Islands. Allow at least two hours — most visitors spend longer than expected. The gift shop sells stunning pieces of polished kauri gum and timber crafts.

reviews.summary: One of the best museums in New Zealand — absolutely fascinating even if you know nothing about kauri. The scale of the timber displays is jaw-dropping. Allow more time than you think you'll need.

pois.name: Waitangi Museum Te Kōngahu
pois.short: State-of-the-art museum at the Waitangi Treaty Grounds telling the foundational story of modern New Zealand.
pois.commentary: Te Kōngahu Museum of Waitangi is a world-class museum within the Waitangi Treaty Grounds, opened in 2016 to tell the complex, contested, and profound story of the Treaty of Waitangi — the founding document of modern New Zealand. The museum features immersive, interactive exhibitions that present multiple perspectives on the treaty's signing in 1840 and its ongoing significance. State-of-the-art multimedia presentations, original taonga (treasured objects), and deeply personal stories create a powerful and thought-provoking experience. The museum is designed to challenge visitors to think about nationhood, partnership, and justice. Entry is included in the Waitangi Treaty Grounds admission, which also gives access to the Treaty House, Māori meeting house (Te Whare Rūnanga), a 35-metre waka taua (war canoe), and cultural performances. Allow at least two hours for the museum and wider grounds. This is arguably the most important heritage site in New Zealand and an essential visit for understanding the country.

reviews.summary: A powerful, moving, and essential New Zealand experience. The museum tells the treaty story with honesty and sophistication. Combined with the wider grounds and cultural performances, it's unmissable.

pois.name: Matapouri Beach & Mermaid Pools
pois.short: Pristine white sand beach with the famous Mermaid Pools — natural rock pools with crystal-clear water.
pois.commentary: Matapouri Beach is one of Northland's most beautiful east coast beaches, a crescent of pure white sand lapped by vivid blue-green water in the Tutukaka Coast area. The beach is famous for the Mermaid Pools (Whakaruakōpako), natural rock pools at the southern end that fill with crystal-clear seawater at low to mid tide, creating magical natural swimming pools. The pools are accessed via a short walk over the headland from the southern end of the beach. Matapouri itself offers safe swimming and good snorkelling around the rocky margins. The beach is backed by a small, quiet community with limited facilities. The nearby Tutukaka coastline is renowned for diving at the Poor Knights Islands Marine Reserve, one of the world's top subtropical dive sites. Matapouri is about 30 minutes from Whangārei. Note that the Mermaid Pools are only accessible around low tide and have cultural significance to local hapū — please treat the area with respect and follow any signage.

reviews.summary: The Mermaid Pools are magical — like natural infinity pools overlooking the ocean. The beach itself is gorgeous too. Check the tides before you go and treat the area with respect.

pois.name: Bland Bay
pois.short: Secluded and beautiful Northland beach with calm swimming, far from the tourist trail.
pois.commentary: Bland Bay is a small, quiet beach settlement on Northland's east coast, south of Whananaki, offering a genuinely remote and peaceful beach experience. Despite its unremarkable name, the beach is a lovely sheltered cove with gentle swimming conditions and clear water. The bay is surrounded by farmland and native bush, with a small community of holiday homes and permanent residents. The beach has a boat ramp popular with recreational fishers, and the calm waters make it suitable for kayaking and paddleboarding. Bland Bay sits within a string of beautiful east coast beaches along the Tutukaka Coast and makes a great alternative to the busier spots further south. There are no shops or facilities, so bring everything you need including drinking water. The gravel road in is well maintained but winding. The lack of development and commercial tourism gives Bland Bay an authentic, off-grid Northland beach experience that is increasingly rare and valuable.

reviews.summary: A beautifully quiet beach where you can have the sand almost to yourself. Not glamorous but that's the appeal — real, unspoilt Northland. Bring supplies as there's nothing nearby.

pois.name: Mangawhai Heads Beach
pois.short: Stunning surf beach with the famous Mangawhai Cliff Walk offering panoramic ocean and estuary views.
pois.commentary: Mangawhai Heads Beach is one of Northland's premier surf and swimming beaches, a long stretch of golden sand backed by impressive sandstone headlands. The beach offers reliable surf breaks popular with boardriders, while the sheltered estuary side provides safe swimming for families. The famous Mangawhai Cliff Walk begins at the northern end of the beach, traversing the headland with spectacular views of the Hen and Chicken Islands, the open Pacific, and the Mangawhai estuary below. The walk takes about an hour each way and passes through coastal bush with a lookout platform at the highest point. Mangawhai has evolved from a sleepy Northland settlement into a popular weekend destination with excellent cafés, a Saturday farmers market, and a thriving food scene. The beach is about 90 minutes north of Auckland, making it accessible for day trips or weekends. Surf lifeguards patrol during summer. The cliff walk and beach combination make this one of Northland's best half-day experiences.

reviews.summary: The cliff walk combined with the beach makes this a perfect half-day outing. Views from the headland are spectacular. Mangawhai village has great food too — a real up-and-coming destination.

pois.name: Parasail Bay of Islands
pois.short: Parasailing flights above the Bay of Islands with views across 144 islands from up to 400 feet.
pois.commentary: Parasailing over the Bay of Islands offers a bird's-eye perspective of one of New Zealand's most beautiful maritime landscapes. Launching from a purpose-built boat, you are gently lifted up to 400 feet above the water, suspended beneath a colourful canopy with panoramic views across the 144 islands, turquoise channels, and the green hills of the mainland. The experience is surprisingly serene at altitude, with time to take in the geography and spot marine life below. Flights typically last 8-10 minutes in the air, with the full experience including boat ride taking about an hour. Solo, tandem, and triple flights are available, making it accessible to most ages and fitness levels. Departures are from Paihia wharf, weather dependent. A unique way to appreciate the scale and beauty of this iconic Northland destination.

reviews.summary: Breathtaking aerial views of the Bay of Islands that photos cannot capture. The takeoff and landing are smooth and gentle. An exhilarating yet peaceful experience suitable for most ages.

pois.name: Cape Brett Lighthouse Track
pois.short: Multi-day coastal walk to a historic 1910 lighthouse at the tip of Cape Brett peninsula, Bay of Islands.
pois.commentary: The Cape Brett Track is a challenging 16km one-way coastal walk along the spine of the Cape Brett peninsula to the historic lighthouse at its tip, overlooking the famous Hole in the Rock (Motukokako). Built in 1910, the lighthouse was automated in 1978, and the former keeper's quarters now serve as a DOC hut for overnight trampers. The track traverses regenerating bush, exposed ridgelines, and steep terrain with spectacular views across the Bay of Islands. The walk is rated as an advanced tramping track and should not be underestimated; carry sufficient water and allow a full day each way. A water taxi option from Paihia or Russell to the cape end shortens the trip. DOC hut bookings are essential during summer. The lighthouse and its dramatic clifftop position make the effort deeply rewarding.

reviews.summary: A challenging but spectacular coastal walk with an unforgettable lighthouse destination. The views from Cape Brett are some of Northland's finest. The DOC hut is basic but the setting is extraordinary.

pois.name: Kerikeri Food Trail
pois.short: Self-drive food and artisan trail through subtropical Kerikeri featuring orchards, chocolatiers, and producers.
pois.commentary: Kerikeri and the surrounding Inlet area have become Northland's food and artisan hub, with a concentration of growers, makers, and producers capitalising on the region's warm subtropical climate. A self-drive trail takes in citrus and avocado orchards, the famous Makana Confections chocolate factory, local olive oil pressers, honey producers, and boutique food stores. The Kerikeri Farmers Market on Sunday mornings is a highlight, bringing together local growers and artisan food makers. The region's volcanic soils and mild winters produce exceptional subtropical fruits including oranges, mandarins, tamarillos, and feijoas. Several cafes and restaurants along the trail champion local produce. The trail connects naturally with Kerikeri's historic attractions including the Stone Store and Mission House, New Zealand's oldest surviving European buildings. Pick up a trail map from the i-SITE visitor centre.

reviews.summary: A delicious way to explore Kerikeri's thriving food scene. The variety of artisan producers is impressive for a small town. The Makana chocolate factory is a standout stop.

pois.name: Manea Footprints of Kupe
pois.short: Immersive storytelling experience at Opononi telling the story of Kupe's arrival in Aotearoa at the Hokianga.
pois.commentary: Manea Footprints of Kupe is an immersive cultural experience in Opononi on the shores of the Hokianga Harbour, telling the story of the great Polynesian navigator Kupe's arrival in Aotearoa New Zealand. The experience uses a combination of theatrical storytelling, multimedia projections, carved panels, and guided narration to bring to life the epic ocean voyage from Hawaiki to the Hokianga. The Hokianga Harbour is one of the most significant sites in Maori oral history, traditionally regarded as one of the first landing places of the ancestral voyaging waka. The purpose-built venue was developed by local hapu (sub-tribes) to share their history on their own terms. The 90-minute experience is led by local guides who connect the ancestral stories to the living community. The views across the Hokianga to the giant sand dunes add a powerful visual context. A deeply moving cultural experience that connects visitors to the origins of Maori settlement in New Zealand.

reviews.summary: A profoundly moving and beautifully told cultural experience. The storytelling is world-class and the community ownership gives it deep authenticity. The Hokianga setting adds emotional weight to the narrative.

pois.name: Waitangi Treaty Grounds
pois.short: The birthplace of modern New Zealand, where the Treaty of Waitangi was signed in 1840 between Maori and the Crown.
pois.commentary: The Waitangi Treaty Grounds are New Zealand's most historically significant site, where on 6 February 1840, Maori chiefs and representatives of the British Crown signed the Treaty of Waitangi, establishing the foundation of modern New Zealand governance. The grounds occupy a beautiful headland overlooking the Bay of Islands and include the Treaty House (built in 1832 as the residence of British Resident James Busby), the whare runanga (meeting house) with carvings representing all Maori tribes, and the world's largest ceremonial war canoe (waka taua), Ngatokimatawhaorua, carved from three giant kauri trees. The modern Museum of Waitangi presents the treaty story through immersive exhibitions exploring perspectives of both signatories. Guided tours and cultural performances including a powerful haka are available throughout the day. Waitangi Day on 6 February is New Zealand's national day, marked by celebrations and commemorations at the grounds. Located five minutes from Paihia in the Bay of Islands.

reviews.summary: Essential visiting for understanding New Zealand's founding document and bicultural history. The guided tours are excellent and the cultural performances are powerful. The grounds and museum deserve at least two hours.

pois.name: Waipoua Forest & Tane Mahuta
pois.short: Home of Tane Mahuta, the largest living kauri tree, in ancient Northland forest sacred to Maori.
pois.commentary: Waipoua Forest is the largest remaining tract of native kauri forest in New Zealand, protecting ancient trees that were already centuries old when Europeans first arrived. The forest's most famous resident is Tane Mahuta (Lord of the Forest), the largest living kauri tree in New Zealand, standing 51.5 metres tall with a trunk girth of 13.77 metres, estimated to be between 1,200 and 2,500 years old. A short boardwalk from the roadside car park leads to a viewing platform at the base of this extraordinary tree, whose presence is genuinely awe-inspiring. Nearby, Te Matua Ngahere (Father of the Forest) is the widest kauri, with a trunk girth of 16.41 metres. Evening guided walks with local Maori guides offer a spiritual and ecological perspective on the forest. Kauri dieback disease is a serious threat, so visitors must use the shoe-cleaning stations provided at every track entrance. Located on SH12 between Dargaville and Opononi.

reviews.summary: Standing before Tane Mahuta is a profound experience. The scale and age of these ancient trees is humbling. The evening guided walks add cultural depth. Always use the shoe-cleaning stations to protect the kauri.

pois.name: Cape Brett Track / Oke Bay to Cape Brett
pois.short: Spectacular overnight coastal track to the historic Cape Brett Lighthouse.
pois.commentary: The Cape Brett Track is one of Northland's most rewarding multi-day walks, traversing the rugged peninsula that forms the eastern arm of the Bay of Islands. The track covers approximately sixteen kilometres from Oke Bay to the Cape Brett Lighthouse, passing through regenerating bush with stunning views of islands, bays, and open ocean. The lighthouse at Cape Brett, built in 1910, sits on a dramatic headland and the DOC hut nearby provides overnight accommodation. The walk is moderately challenging with several steep sections. Book the hut well in advance during summer. A water taxi return to Rawhiti or Russell can be arranged, avoiding the need to retrace the full track. Permit required from DOC. The marine environment around the cape is rich with dolphins and seabirds.

reviews.summary: A stunning coastal walk to a dramatic lighthouse setting. The views are world-class. Book the hut early and arrange a water taxi return.

pois.name: Rainbow Falls / Waianiwaniwa
pois.short: 27m waterfall near Kerikeri — beautiful bush walk and swimming hole. Best after rain when the falls are full.
pois.commentary: Rainbow Falls / Waianiwaniwa drops 27 metres over a basalt ledge on the Kerikeri River, creating rainbows in the mist on sunny mornings — hence the name. The walk from Waipapa Road takes about 15 minutes through native bush, passing a swimming hole popular in summer. You can also approach from the Kerikeri end via the longer Kerikeri River Track, which passes the historic Stone Store and Mission House. After heavy rain the falls are spectacular, but the swimming hole is best on calmer days.

reviews.summary: Visitors enjoy the easy access and photogenic falls. The swimming hole is a summer favourite. Combining with the Kerikeri heritage walk is recommended.

pois.name: Whangārei Falls
pois.short: A perfect 26m curtain waterfall surrounded by native bush — one of NZ's most photographed falls.
pois.commentary: Whangārei Falls is postcard-perfect — a broad 26-metre curtain of water dropping over a basalt ledge into a deep pool, framed by native bush and nikau palms. The upper lookout is just minutes from the car park, but the loop track down to the base (20 minutes) gives you the full picture. The falls are at their most dramatic after rain, and in summer the pool below is swimmable (with care). Tūī, kererū, and pūkeko are common in the surrounding reserve. It's a short detour from SH1 and well worth the stop.

reviews.summary: One of the most frequently praised stops in Northland. The easy access and photogenic nature of the falls are highlighted. The loop walk is short enough for any fitness level.

pois.name: Pompallier Mission, Russell
pois.short: NZ's oldest industrial building — the 1842 French Catholic mission where the first books were printed in Māori.
pois.commentary: Pompallier Mission is a remarkable survivor — built in 1842 by French Marist missionaries using rammed earth (pisé) construction, it is New Zealand's oldest surviving industrial building. Inside, the original printing press and tannery were used to produce religious texts in te reo Māori, making this one of the earliest sites of Māori-language publishing. The waterfront location in Russell (then the capital, Kororāreka) is beautiful, and guided tours bring the mission's story vividly to life. Heritage New Zealand manages the site with care and authenticity.

reviews.summary: History enthusiasts rate the guided tour highly for its depth and storytelling. The rammed earth construction technique fascinates visitors. The Russell waterfront setting adds to the atmosphere.

pois.name: Kauri Museum, Matakohe
pois.short: The definitive museum of NZ's kauri heritage — timber industry, gum digging, and stunning furniture.
pois.commentary: The Kauri Museum at Matakohe is unexpectedly magnificent — a community-run museum that tells the complete story of New Zealand's kauri industry with a depth and quality that rivals any national institution. The centrepiece is a cross-section of a kauri trunk so large it fills a room. Surrounding it are displays on the timber milling industry, the Dalmatian gum diggers, pioneer life, and some of the finest kauri furniture ever made. The volunteer guides are passionate and knowledgeable. Allow two hours minimum — most visitors stay longer.

reviews.summary: Consistently rated as one of New Zealand's best regional museums. Visitors are amazed by the scale of the kauri displays. The community-run nature adds warmth and authenticity.

pois.name: Poor Knights Islands Marine Reserve
pois.short: Jacques Cousteau ranked this among the top 10 dive sites in the world — subtropical waters, sea caves, and extraordinary marine life.
pois.commentary: The Poor Knights Islands sit in a subtropical convergence zone where warm currents bring species found nowhere else in New Zealand — subtropical fish, black coral, and massive kelp forests. Jacques Cousteau reportedly ranked it among the world's top ten dive sites. The islands themselves are a nature reserve — no landing permitted — but the surrounding waters are accessible by dive boat from Tutukaka. The sea caves and archways are enormous, with Rikoriko Cave being the world's largest known sea cave. Even snorkellers see extraordinary marine life in the shallows.

reviews.summary: Divers rate the Poor Knights as world-class without hesitation. The marine biodiversity astonishes even experienced divers. Boat trips from Tutukaka are well-organised and professional.

pois.name: Mangōnui Fish Shop & Harbour
pois.short: Possibly NZ's most famous fish and chip shop — fresh fish eaten on the wharf overlooking Doubtless Bay.
pois.commentary: The Mangōnui Fish Shop has been serving fresh fish and chips from its wharf-side location since the 1960s, and it has become a genuine New Zealand institution. The fish is landed locally — often snapper, tarakihi, or blue cod — battered and fried to order, then eaten sitting on the wharf with your legs dangling over Doubtless Bay. The harbour village of Mangōnui is charming in its own right: old wooden buildings, a historic courthouse, and a gentle pace that feels like New Zealand 40 years ago.

reviews.summary: The wharf-side fish and chips experience is rated as quintessentially Kiwi. Freshness of the fish is consistently praised. The Mangōnui village itself is a pleasant surprise for many visitors.

pois.name: Ruapekapeka Pā
pois.short: Site of the last battle of the Northern War (1846) — remarkably preserved earthworks and trenches that influenced global military design.
pois.commentary: Ruapekapeka Pā was the site of the final engagement of the Northern War on 11 January 1846, and its innovative design makes it one of the most significant military sites in New Zealand. Chief Kawiti built a fortification so sophisticated that British officers later studied it — anti-artillery bunkers, firing trenches, and communication tunnels that predated European trench warfare by decades. The earthworks are remarkably well-preserved and interpreted with excellent DOC panels. Walking through the trenches gives a visceral sense of the ingenuity of Māori military engineering.

reviews.summary: History enthusiasts are consistently impressed by the sophistication of the pā design. The DOC interpretation panels are rated excellent. A powerful and under-visited site.

pois.name: Waipoua Forest & Tāne Mahuta
pois.short: Home of Tāne Mahuta, lord of the forest — NZ's largest known kauri tree, estimated 2,000+ years old.
pois.commentary: Tāne Mahuta stands 51 metres tall with a girth of nearly 14 metres — the largest known living kauri tree in New Zealand, estimated to be between 1,250 and 2,500 years old. Named after the Māori god of the forest, the tree is genuinely awe-inspiring: standing at its base, you feel the scale of deep time. The short boardwalk from SH12 takes five minutes. Nearby, Te Matua Ngahere ('father of the forest') is shorter but even wider. Kauri dieback disease means strict hygiene — use the shoe-cleaning stations at every track entrance. The night tours with local Māori guides are exceptional.

reviews.summary: Visitors universally describe standing before Tāne Mahuta as a humbling, spiritual experience. The night tours with Māori guides are rated outstanding. Shoe hygiene stations are essential — take kauri dieback seriously.

pois.name: Urupukapuka Island
pois.short: The largest island in the Bay of Islands — walking tracks, pristine beaches, and archaeological sites.
pois.commentary: Urupukapuka is the only island in the Bay of Islands with a public walking track network, and it's a gem. The island loop walk (about three hours) crosses grassy headlands with views across the bay to Russell and the mainland, passing secluded beaches perfect for swimming. Archaeological sites include pā terraces and middens dating back centuries. The DOC campsite is basic but beautifully situated. Ferries run from Paihia and Russell, and you can combine the island with a dolphin-watching cruise. Pack lunch — there's a small café in summer only.

reviews.summary: Walkers and beach lovers rate Urupukapuka as a Bay of Islands highlight. The combination of walking, swimming, and history is praised. Ferry access makes it easy as a day trip from Paihia.

pois.name: Hundertwasser Art Centre Whangarei
pois.short: The only Hundertwasser building in the Southern Hemisphere — an extraordinary art centre completed in 2022 in central Whangarei.
pois.commentary: Friedensreich Hundertwasser designed this building in 1993, designating Whangarei as the sole Southern Hemisphere location for his organic, anti-straight-line architecture. It took nearly 30 years of community fundraising to realise. Opened in 2022, the building is extraordinary — undulating floors, ceramic tile columns, golden onion domes, tree tenants growing from the roof, and not a straight angle in sight. It houses three gallery spaces including the Wairau Māori Art Gallery (Northland's finest collection of contemporary Māori art), rotating national exhibitions, and a glass-blowing studio. The building alone is worth stopping in Whangarei even if simply passing through.

reviews.summary: An architectural revelation. The 30-year wait was worth it — the building exceeds expectations in every dimension. The Wairau Gallery inside is outstanding. A must-see even with limited time in Northland.

pois.name: Whangarei Quarry Arts Centre
pois.short: A former volcanic quarry transformed into an arts village — studios, galleries, and sculpture gardens in a striking landscape.
pois.commentary: The Whangarei Quarry Arts Centre occupies an old volcanic rock quarry on the edge of the city and has evolved into one of Northland's most creative spaces over 40 years. Over 30 studios house working artists — potters, weavers, jewellers, glassblowers, and printmakers — most open for visitors to watch and buy directly. The quarry walls form a dramatic backdrop to the sculpture garden. The Sunday market draws locals and visitors alike. Entry is free. The complex is a 10-minute walk from the Town Basin marina precinct — an excellent combination with the Hundertwasser Art Centre and Whangarei Museum.

reviews.summary: A genuine working arts community in a spectacular volcanic quarry setting. Watching artists work in their studios is engaging and the Sunday market is excellent.

pois.name: Whangarei Town Basin and Claphams Clock Museum
pois.short: Whangarei's vibrant waterfront precinct — galleries, the famous Claphams Clock Museum, craft shops, and marina dining.
pois.commentary: Whangarei's Town Basin is the city's cultural anchor — a marina-side precinct built around the historic Claphams Clock Museum, which holds New Zealand's largest collection of working clocks (over 1,400 in a Victorian building that ticks and chimes on the quarter hour). The Basin also houses the Northland Society of Arts gallery, ceramic and jewellery studios, Reyburn House gallery, and marina-side restaurants and cafes. The Hātea River loop walk from the Basin gives a 3.5km walkthrough of the developing inner city with public art installations throughout.

reviews.summary: Claphams Clock Museum is a delightful surprise — the chiming and ticking is hypnotic. The Basin precinct is well-developed and pleasant for an extended visit. Good base for exploring central Whangarei.

pois.name: Kerikeri Arts Precinct and Studio Trail
pois.short: Kerikeri has one of NZ's highest concentrations of working artists — studios, galleries, and artisan workshops open to visitors.
pois.commentary: Kerikeri's exceptional climate, natural beauty, and heritage setting have attracted artists, craftspeople, and makers for decades. The town and surrounding district has over 100 working artists, with a formal studio trail published by Arts Bay of Islands. Highlights include Moffat's Gallery (established Northland painters), Earthworks Pottery, several jewellery studios, and the Kerikeri Artisan Market (every Saturday morning at Hobson Avenue). The village is also the gateway to the Stone Store Basin heritage precinct — combining the arts and heritage trails makes for a full and rewarding day.

reviews.summary: The concentration of quality artists in the Kerikeri area is genuinely remarkable. The studio trail is rewarding and the artisan market is excellent. The combination with the Stone Store heritage precinct makes for a wonderful full day.

pois.name: Manea — Footprints of Kupe, Opononi
pois.short: A remarkable cultural immersion experience telling the story of Kupe's discovery of Aotearoa — at the Hokianga, where he first landed.
pois.commentary: Manea is built on the whenua where Kupe, the great Polynesian navigator, is believed to have first arrived in Aotearoa. The cultural experience centre at Opononi uses cutting-edge projection technology, te reo Māori narration, and taonga to tell the story of the great double-hull waka that first found these islands. The 90-minute guided experience is developed with Ngāpuhi iwi and integrates seamlessly with the Hokianga's landscape — the massive sand dunes visible across the harbour from Opononi are part of the same story. One of the most thoughtfully developed Māori cultural experiences in Northland, and far less visited than Waitangi. Advance booking essential.

reviews.summary: An outstanding cultural experience — emotionally resonant, technically impressive, and deeply grounded in Ngāpuhi knowledge. The Hokianga setting adds immense context. One of the most memorable experiences in all of Northland.

pois.name: Twin Coast Discovery Highway — Full Circuit
pois.short: The Twin Coast Discovery Highway loops Northland — Pacific coast to Tasman coast, crossing the full peninsula.
pois.commentary: The Twin Coast Discovery Highway is Northland's definitive road experience — a 500km circuit that traverses the full peninsula from Auckland via the Pacific (SH1) coast to the Bay of Islands and Kerikeri, crosses to the Hokianga Harbour, runs the kauri coast through Waipoua, and returns via Dargaville and the Kaipara Harbour. The circuit visits virtually every major attraction in Northland: Waitangi, the Stone Store, Cape Reinga, the kauri forests, the Hokianga dunes, and the Kauri Museum. A minimum of three days is required; five is better. The self-drive trail is well signposted with its own dedicated app and guidebook.

reviews.summary: The definitive Northland road experience. The breadth of landscape — Pacific coast to Hokianga — is extraordinary. Every traveller to Northland should do at least a section of this route.

pois.name: Ninety Mile Beach Drive (Te Oneroa-a-Tōhē)
pois.short: The legendary 88km beach drive along Te Oneroa-a-Tōhē — a certified public road and one of NZ's most extraordinary driving experiences.
pois.commentary: Despite the name, Ninety Mile Beach runs approximately 88km — the longest driveable beach in New Zealand. The beach is a legally classified public road, and coach tours and 4WD vehicles regularly transit it between Kaitāia and Cape Reinga. The drive must be timed to low tide — the beach is tidal and vehicles have been lost to incoming water and quicksand. The sand is hard-packed and drives like a sealed road when dry. The towering Aupouri Peninsula sand dunes loom to one side; the open Tasman pounds the shore to the other. Te Oneroa-a-Tōhē has deep significance for Ngāti Kuri and Te Rarawa — the beach is a traditional mahinga kai and the route walked by spirits to Cape Reinga.

reviews.summary: One of New Zealand's most memorable driving experiences. The scale is genuinely impressive. Tide timing is critical — go with a guided tour or do thorough research beforehand.

pois.name: Far North Loop — Cape Reinga to Hokianga
pois.short: The Far North loop from Cape Reinga through the Aupouri Peninsula to the Hokianga — two days of dramatic and remote New Zealand.
pois.commentary: North of Kaitāia, the Aupouri Peninsula narrows to a thin strip between two coasts and becomes one of the most remote areas of the North Island. The loop takes in Cape Reinga lighthouse (where spirits depart for the underworld in Māori belief), Te Paki sand dunes (epic sandboarding), Spirits Bay, and descends the west coast through Ahipara to the Hokianga Harbour. The journey is far better as two days — staying at Kaitāia or Te Hapua allows early morning light at the Cape. Roads are partly unsealed; 4WD is advisable for beach sections. One of New Zealand's most spiritually charged landscape journeys.

reviews.summary: The most remote and moving road experience in Northland. Cape Reinga at dawn is unforgettable. The loop through Spirits Bay and Te Paki deserves more time than most visitors give it.

pois.name: Bay of Islands Full-Day Island Cruise
pois.short: A day-cruise through the 144 islands of the Bay of Islands — swimming coves, historic Russell, and the iconic Hole in the Rock.
pois.commentary: The Bay of Islands contains 144 islands and several operators offer full and half-day cruises from Paihia. The classic full-day cruise visits Moturua Island (walking track, snorkelling), the Hole in the Rock (Motukōkako — the sea cave at Piercy Island through which the boat passes if conditions allow), and Russell (Kororāreka — NZ's first permanent European settlement). Dolphin watching is included on most trips; swimming with dolphins is available on specialist tours. The bay's 2,000km of coastline, sheltered anchorages, and extraordinary water clarity made it the centre of European-Māori contact in the early 19th century — the landscape and its history are inseparable.

reviews.summary: The Hole in the Rock transit is a genuine thrill. Dolphin encounters are frequent and the water clarity is extraordinary. A full day on the water is the best way to appreciate the scale of the Bay.

pois.name: Waipoua–Waima Kauri Forest Drive (SH12)
pois.short: SH12 through Waipoua and Waima forests — the most impressive drive through surviving ancient kauri country in Northland.
pois.commentary: State Highway 12 through Waipoua Forest is the most significant forest drive in Northland — 11,500 hectares of the largest surviving kauri forest in the world. The drive from Dargaville to Opononi takes 90 minutes but should take four hours if you allow time for the short walks to Tāne Mahuta (the country's largest living kauri tree) and Te Matua Ngahere (the oldest). The road is sealed throughout and winds through dark, cathedral-like forest. Kauri dieback disease protocols require all visitors to clean footwear at hygiene stations on the tracks — critical and non-negotiable. Extending the drive to the Hokianga Harbour makes one of Northland's most complete days.
sources_to_check: Te Roroa iwi — kaitiaki of Waipoua

reviews.summary: A profoundly moving forest experience. Standing beneath Tāne Mahuta is unlike anything else in New Zealand. The drive itself is beautiful. Please comply with kauri dieback protocols — these trees are irreplaceable.

pois.name: Hokianga Harbour Ferry — Rawene to Kohukohu
pois.short: The Hokianga Harbour car ferry from Rawene to Kohukohu — a slow, beautiful 15-minute crossing connecting Northland's two coasts.
pois.commentary: The Rawene-Kohukohu car ferry crosses the Hokianga Harbour in 15 minutes and saves a 70km road detour. But the crossing is far more than a shortcut — the view from the middle of the harbour, looking south to the towering sand dunes and north to forested hills, is one of the finest in Northland. Rawene is a tiny, perfectly preserved colonial port town (Clendon House, the oldest house in Northland still on its original site) and Kohukohu is a remarkably intact Victorian village. The ferry runs daily 8am–5:30pm; no booking required. Coming from the Waipoua kauri forest, the ferry is the natural and most beautiful way to continue the circuit into the Bay of Islands.

reviews.summary: A small pleasure that stays with you. The harbour view from the ferry is extraordinary. Both Rawene and Kohukohu reward a short wander. One of those NZ moments that happens between the headline attractions.

pois.name: Poor Knights Islands Marine Reserve Dive Journey
pois.short: Jacques Cousteau named the Poor Knights among the world's top ten dive sites — the marine reserve is NZ's finest underwater experience.
pois.commentary: The Poor Knights Islands Marine Reserve, 24km off the Tutukaka coast, was designated one of the world's top ten dive sites by Jacques Cousteau. Subtropical currents, sea caves, archways, and cliffs plunging 100m underwater host an extraordinary density of marine life. The islands have been uninhabited since a 1820s raid; a protective tapu is maintained by Ngāti Wai. Day trips depart from Tutukaka Marina with equipment for hire. The Rikoriko Cave — the world's largest accessible sea cave — can be entered without scuba. Snorkellers see blue maomao schools, trevally, eagle rays, and nudibranchs in the shallows. One of New Zealand's unmissable natural experiences.

reviews.summary: The best marine experience in New Zealand and one of the best in the world. The diversity and density of life is extraordinary even for snorkellers. Worth planning your entire Northland visit around.

pois.name: Poor Knights Islands Marine Reserve
pois.short: World-class diving at subtropical islands rated among the top 10 dive sites globally.
pois.commentary: The Poor Knights Islands Marine Reserve, located 23 kilometres off the Tutukaka coast, is consistently ranked among the world's top ten dive sites. Jacques Cousteau rated it one of the top five. The islands sit at the convergence of subtropical and temperate currents, creating extraordinary biodiversity with over 125 fish species. The underwater terrain features caves, arches, and tunnels, including the famous Rikoriko — the world's largest known sea cave. Dive boats depart from Tutukaka Marina.

reviews.summary: World-class diving that lives up to the hype. Rikoriko Cave is breathtaking. Even snorkelling reveals incredible marine life.

pois.name: Twin Coast Discovery Highway
pois.short: Circular scenic route linking Northland's east and west coasts.
pois.commentary: The Twin Coast Discovery Highway is a circular touring route around Northland, connecting the Pacific coast beaches of the east with the dramatic Tasman Sea coastline of the west. The route passes through major attractions including the Bay of Islands, Kerikeri, the Waitangi Treaty Grounds, the Kauri Museum at Matakohe, and Waipoua Forest's ancient kauri trees. The full circuit from Auckland and back covers around 800 kilometres and ideally needs 4-5 days.

reviews.summary: The best way to see Northland properly. The east-west coastal contrast is striking. Allow more time than you think.

pois.name: Manea – Footprints of Kupe
pois.short: Immersive experience telling the story of Polynesian navigator Kupe's arrival in Aotearoa.
pois.commentary: Manea – Footprints of Kupe is an immersive cultural experience in Opononi on the Hokianga Harbour, telling the story of the great Polynesian navigator Kupe's first arrival in Aotearoa through a combination of carved taonga, digital storytelling, film, and guided interpretation. The experience is owned and operated by the local hapu of Te Hikutu and Te Whanau Pani, making it an authentic indigenous-led cultural attraction. The Hokianga Harbour itself provides a dramatic natural setting.

reviews.summary: A moving, beautifully told story of Polynesian navigation. Hapu-owned and authentic. The Hokianga setting adds power to the narrative.

pois.name: Bay of Islands Cruise / Hole in the Rock
pois.short: Scenic cruise through 144 islands to the famous Hole in the Rock at Cape Brett.
pois.commentary: The Bay of Islands cruise to the Hole in the Rock is one of Northland's quintessential experiences, weaving through 144 subtropical islands to the dramatic Cape Brett headland where a natural rock arch at Motukōkako allows boats to pass through in calm conditions. En route, the boat passes deserted sandy beaches, historic sites, and marine wildlife. Dolphins — both bottlenose and common — are frequently spotted and sometimes bow-ride alongside the vessel. The Cape Brett Lighthouse stands sentinel above the Hole in the Rock. Several operators run daily departures from Paihia and Russell, with options ranging from full-day cream trips (visiting inner islands) to faster catamaran direct routes. The Bay of Islands' sheltered waters make for comfortable cruising even on moderately rough days.

reviews.summary: Stunning island scenery and reliable dolphin sightings. The Hole in the Rock is dramatic. Cream trip option adds historical interest.

pois.name: Tutukaka Coast
pois.short: Stunning coastline with sheltered bays, dive sites, and the gateway to the Poor Knights.
pois.commentary: The Tutukaka Coast east of Whangarei is one of Northland's most beautiful stretches of coastline, featuring golden-sand beaches in sheltered coves, rocky headlands, and warm subtropical waters. The coast is the access point for the Poor Knights Islands Marine Reserve — one of the world's top dive sites — and Tutukaka Marina is the departure point for dive boats and fishing charters. Matapouri Bay and Whale Bay are standout swimming beaches, with the Mermaid Pool (a natural tidal pool carved into the rock) at Matapouri being a local favourite. Sandy Bay is a popular surf break. The coast is relatively undeveloped, with small-scale accommodation and a few excellent restaurants. The drive along the coast from Ngunguru to Tutukaka provides regular viewpoints over the dramatic coastline.

reviews.summary: Beautiful, uncrowded coastline with excellent swimming beaches. The gateway to world-class diving. Mermaid Pool is a hidden treasure.
