pois.name: Cape Reinga / Te Rerenga Wairua
pois.short: NZ's spiritual northern tip where Māori spirits depart for the afterlife — a profoundly significant site.
pois.commentary: Cape Reinga (Te Rerenga Wairua — 'the leaping place of spirits') is the most spiritually significant site in northern New Zealand. In Māori belief, the spirits of the dead travel north along the length of the country to this point, where they descend a pōhutukawa tree on the headland and enter the sea to journey to Hawaiki, the ancestral homeland. The site is marked by a lighthouse on the dramatic headland where the Tasman Sea and Pacific Ocean visibly collide. The cultural significance is palpable — visitors often describe an emotional response to the place. The drive to Cape Reinga along the Aupouri Peninsula passes through remote landscapes and the vast Te Paki sand dunes.

reviews.summary: A profoundly spiritual and visually dramatic place. The meeting of two oceans is visible. Travel with cultural awareness.

pois.name: Russell / Kororāreka Historic Town
pois.short: NZ's first capital — a charming historic town with colonial heritage and Māori significance.
pois.commentary: Russell (originally Kororāreka) was New Zealand's first European settlement and briefly served as the country's capital. The town's history is colourful — it was known as the 'hell hole of the Pacific' in the whaling era, witnessed the signing of the Treaty of Waitangi nearby, and was the site of Hōne Heke's famous flagstaff wars. Today, Russell is a charming, beautifully preserved historic town accessible by ferry from Paihia. Key sites include Christ Church (New Zealand's oldest surviving church, with musket-ball holes from the 1845 battle), Pompallier Mission (the oldest industrial building), and the Russell Museum. The ferry crossing from Paihia adds to the experience.

reviews.summary: Beautifully preserved historic town with fascinating stories. Christ Church's bullet holes bring history alive. The ferry crossing is scenic.

pois.name: Ninety Mile Beach / Te Oneroa-a-Tōhē
pois.short: Drive 88km along a vast ocean beach — one of NZ's most epic road trips (4WD recommended).
pois.commentary: Ninety Mile Beach (actually 88km) is a vast, unbroken stretch of ocean beach on Northland's west coast that can be driven at low tide. The experience of driving along this seemingly endless beach — surf crashing on one side, sand dunes on the other — is genuinely epic. The beach is legally classified as a highway, though rental car insurance typically doesn't cover beach driving (4WD tour operators are the safest option). The Te Paki Sand Dunes at the northern end offer sandboarding down massive dunes into freshwater streams. The beach connects to Cape Reinga via a stream crossing. The scale of the landscape — the immense beach, the towering dunes, the endless ocean — creates a sense of space and freedom that's addictive.

reviews.summary: An epic, unforgettable experience. The sheer scale is overwhelming. Use a tour operator — rental car insurance doesn't cover it.

pois.name: Matapouri Bay & Mermaid Pools
pois.short: Hidden tidal rock pools reached by a short coastal walk from a stunning white-sand beach.
pois.commentary: The Mermaid Pools at Matapouri Bay are a set of natural rock pools carved into the coastal rocks, reached by a short (15-minute) walk from the beautiful white-sand beach. The pools fill at high tide and retain clear, calm water as the tide drops, creating natural swimming pools of crystal-clear sea water. The setting is dramatic — dark volcanic rock, white water surging over the reef, and views along the Tutukaka Coast. Matapouri Bay itself is one of Northland's most beautiful beaches. The pools are best visited on an incoming or recently full tide for the deepest water. They can be slippery — take care on the rocks.

reviews.summary: Beautiful natural rock pools in a stunning coastal setting. Matapouri Beach is gorgeous too. Best at high tide. Take care on wet rocks.

pois.name: Hātea River Walk & Town Basin
pois.short: Riverside walk through mangroves and a revitalised town basin with dining and art galleries.
pois.commentary: The Hātea River Walk in Whangārei follows the river from the Town Basin through mangrove forest and parkland — a surprisingly engaging urban walk. The mangrove boardwalk section takes you through the heart of a functioning mangrove ecosystem, with interpretive panels explaining these often-underappreciated trees' ecological importance. The Town Basin itself has been revitalised with restaurants, cafes, art galleries, and a public sculpture park. The Claphams Clock Museum houses one of Australasia's largest clock collections. The walk extends to Whangārei Falls (one of New Zealand's most photogenic waterfalls) for those wanting a longer route. The whole experience demonstrates how a modest city can celebrate its natural and cultural assets.

reviews.summary: Pleasant riverside walk with good mangrove ecology. The Town Basin dining is nice. Extends nicely to Whangārei Falls.

pois.name: Kerikeri Stone Store & Mission House
pois.short: NZ's oldest stone building (1836) and oldest wooden building — cradle of European settlement.
pois.commentary: The Kerikeri Stone Store, built in 1836, is New Zealand's oldest surviving stone building. Adjacent is Kemp House (originally the Mission House), built in 1822 — New Zealand's oldest surviving wooden building. Together, they form the most significant collection of colonial-era buildings in the country, situated on the banks of the Kerikeri Inlet beside a basin overlooked by the Kororipo Pā site. The Stone Store now operates as a museum and shop, its thick walls and original timber interiors intact. The surrounding historic precinct tells the story of early European settlement and the complex relationships between missionaries and Māori. The setting — heritage buildings, river, and pā site — beautifully encapsulates early New Zealand history.

reviews.summary: Historically significant and beautifully presented. NZ's oldest buildings are surprisingly atmospheric. The inlet setting is lovely.

pois.name: Hokianga Harbour & Sand Dunes
pois.short: Dramatic harbour entrance with towering sand dunes — sandboard down and swim in the harbour.
pois.commentary: The Hokianga Harbour is one of Northland's most scenic waterways — a vast tidal harbour flanked by native bush, small Māori communities, and the dramatic sand dunes at its mouth. The dunes, rising over 150 metres at South Head, can be reached by a small ferry from Opononi. Sandboarding down the massive dunes into the warm harbour water is one of Northland's most exhilarating free activities. The harbour has deep cultural significance for Te Roroa and Te Hikutu iwi. The small towns along the harbour — Rawene, Opononi, Omapere — have a wonderfully remote, end-of-the-road character. The harbour sunsets, with the dunes glowing golden, are among the most beautiful in New Zealand.

reviews.summary: Dramatic harbour with towering sand dunes. Sandboarding is brilliant fun. The remoteness adds character. Sunsets are spectacular.

pois.name: Kawiti Glowworm Caves
pois.short: Māori-owned glowworm caves with intimate guided tours.
pois.commentary: The Kawiti Glowworm Caves near Kawakawa offer an intimate, family-run glowworm experience that many visitors prefer to the larger Waitomo caves. Owned and operated by descendants of the renowned Ngāti Hine chief Kawiti, the caves feature thousands of glowworms illuminating stalactites and limestone formations in the darkness. Small group tours are led by family members who share both geological information and the caves' significance to their whānau. The cave system includes tight passages and a stream crossing, adding to the adventure. The experience feels personal and authentic rather than commercial. Located just off SH1 near the famous Hundertwasser toilets in Kawakawa. Tours run regularly throughout the day. Affordable and suitable for all ages.

reviews.summary: Visitors love the personal, family-run feel. The glowworms are spectacular in the intimate cave setting. The Māori heritage stories add depth. Great value.

pois.name: Waitangi Mangrove Boardwalk & Haruru Falls
pois.short: A boardwalk through mangrove forest connecting Paihia to Haruru Falls — a gentle walk through a unique coastal ecosystem.
pois.commentary: The Waitangi Mangrove Boardwalk follows the Waitangi Estuary from Paihia through dense mangrove forest to the horseshoe-shaped Haruru Falls. The boardwalk elevates you above the mangrove roots and tidal channels, offering close views of crabs, mudskippers and wading birds that inhabit this estuarine ecosystem. Mangroves are New Zealand's only tropical tree, growing at their southern limit in Northland, and the interpretive signs explain their ecological importance. The walk takes about 45 minutes each way and ends at the falls, where the river drops over a basalt ledge in a broad horseshoe. An excellent family walk.

reviews.summary: A pleasant walk through an unusual ecosystem. The boardwalk is well-maintained and accessible. Haruru Falls is a worthwhile destination. Best at low tide for wildlife viewing. Easy walking for all ages.

pois.name: Tāne Mahuta Night Experience
pois.short: Experience the Waipoua Forest at night — guided walks to Tāne Mahuta and through the bush listening for kiwi calls.
pois.commentary: The Waipoua Forest takes on a completely different character at night. Guided nocturnal tours led by local Māori guides begin with a visit to Tāne Mahuta — the largest known kauri tree — lit only by the glow of the forest. The party then walks quietly through the bush listening for the piercing call of the North Island brown kiwi, which are common in this forest. The guides weave Māori storytelling and forest ecology together seamlessly, creating an experience that is both educational and deeply atmospheric. Seeing Tāne Mahuta in the darkness, without the daytime crowds, is a genuinely spiritual experience for many visitors.

reviews.summary: An unforgettable experience. The guides are outstanding storytellers. Kiwi are frequently heard and sometimes seen. Tāne Mahuta at night is awe-inspiring. Book well in advance — tours are very popular.

pois.name: Russell Heritage Walking Trail
pois.short: Walk through New Zealand's first European settlement — once known as the 'hell-hole of the Pacific,' now a charming harbour village.
pois.commentary: Russell (originally Kororāreka) was New Zealand's first European settlement and briefly its capital, earning a reputation as the 'hell-hole of the Pacific' for its lawless whaling days. The heritage walk traces this wild history through the town's surviving buildings: Christ Church (NZ's oldest surviving church, with musket holes from the 1845 battle), Pompallier Mission (a French Catholic printing house), and the Duke of Marlborough Hotel (one of NZ's oldest licenced hotels). The contrast between Russell's violent past and its current genteel harbour-village atmosphere is striking. The walk takes about 90 minutes and is self-guided with excellent information panels.

reviews.summary: A fascinating walk through New Zealand's earliest European history. The heritage buildings are well-preserved. The information panels are excellent. The harbour setting is beautiful. Ferry from Paihia adds to the experience.

pois.name: Mahoe Farmhouse Cheese — Kerikeri
pois.short: Family-run farmhouse cheese maker producing award-winning Gouda and Edam.
pois.commentary: Mahoe Farmhouse Cheese in Kerikeri has been handcrafting Dutch-style cheeses from their own herd's milk since 1985. Their range includes Gouda, Edam, and cumin cheese, all available for tasting at the farm gate — a genuine artisan experience in the Bay of Islands.

reviews.summary: Outstanding farmhouse cheese — the aged Gouda is exceptional.

pois.name: Rangihoua Heritage Park
pois.short: Site of New Zealand's first mission station and European settlement.
pois.commentary: Rangihoua Heritage Park marks where Samuel Marsden established New Zealand's first mission station in 1814 and preached the first Christian sermon. The heritage walk passes archaeological remains, orchards, and a memorial cross in a beautiful Bay of Islands coastal setting.

reviews.summary: Deeply significant historic site in a beautiful setting.

pois.name: Waipoua Forest Twilight Encounter
pois.short: Māori-guided twilight walk to encounter ancient kauri giants including Tāne Mahuta.
pois.commentary: Footprints Waipoua offers an immersive twilight encounter guided by local Māori hosts. The evening walk through the ancient kauri forest to Tāne Mahuta and Te Matua Ngahere includes Māori storytelling, karakia (prayers), and encounters with kiwi calls in the darkening forest.

reviews.summary: Profoundly moving experience — the Māori storytelling transforms the visit.

pois.name: Spirits Bay / Piwhane Beach
pois.short: Sacred beach near Cape Reinga where Māori spirits depart for the afterlife.
pois.commentary: Spirits Bay / Piwhane is a sweeping white sand beach near Cape Reinga, sacred in Māori tradition as the place where spirits of the dead begin their journey to Hawaiki. The remote, wind-swept beauty and spiritual significance make this one of New Zealand's most powerful places.

reviews.summary: Hauntingly beautiful — you can feel the spiritual presence.

pois.name: Tutukaka Coast — Poor Knights Islands Dive
pois.short: World-class diving at the Poor Knights Islands marine reserve.
pois.commentary: Jacques Cousteau rated the Poor Knights Islands among the top ten dive sites in the world. The subtropical marine reserve teems with life — massive schools of fish, caves, archways, and kelp forests. Boats depart from the Tutukaka Coast for diving and snorkelling trips.

reviews.summary: World-class diving — the underwater caves and marine life are extraordinary.

pois.name: Rawene Heritage Village & Clendon House
pois.short: Picturesque harbour village with one of New Zealand's oldest surviving colonial homes.
pois.commentary: Rawene is one of New Zealand's oldest European settlements, perched on the Hokianga Harbour. Clendon House (1860s) is one of the country's oldest kauri homes, and the village retains its heritage character with verandah-fronted buildings overlooking the harbour.

reviews.summary: Lovely heritage village with a genuine time-warp feel.

pois.name: Bay of Islands Vintage Railway
pois.short: Vintage steam train journey from Kawakawa through farmland to the coast.
pois.commentary: The Bay of Islands Vintage Railway operates steam locomotive Gabriel and vintage diesel engines on the scenic line from Kawakawa to Opua. The two-hour journey crosses farmland and rivers, departing from Kawakawa — the town famous for its Hundertwasser toilets.

reviews.summary: Charming heritage train ride — the steam locomotive is beautiful.

pois.name: Trounson Kauri Park Night Walk
pois.short: Guided night walk through kauri forest with excellent chances of hearing wild kiwi.
pois.commentary: Trounson Kauri Park is a mainland island sanctuary where intensive predator control has allowed kiwi populations to recover. Night walks through the ancient kauri forest regularly encounter kiwi calls and sightings, along with morepork, glowworms, and native frogs.

reviews.summary: Magical night in the kauri forest — we heard kiwi everywhere.
